ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW (Noodles, fried) is the closest parent category; no specific code exists for Maggi Goreng Seafood in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Maggi Goreng Seafood or fried instant noodle dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Maggi Goreng Seafood as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean hawker stall or casual restaurant, weighing approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Goreng (Fried Noodles with Egg and Vegetables) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists Mee Goreng (fried instant noodles with egg and vegetables) as the closest approved match. Maggi Goreng Seafood is a variant that substitutes mixed seafood for the protein component; macronutrient profile is adapted from the HPB Mee Goreng entry with a modest upward adjustment to protein_g (~+1.5 g/100 g) and sodium_mg (~+30 mg/100 g) to reflect the seafood and additional seasoning. All values are per 100 g of the prepared dish. IMR Malaysia MyFCD 'Mee Goreng' entry was used as a cross-reference to validate the macro range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Instant noodles, cooked / Fried noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Maggi Goreng Seafood. The GI of 53 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked instant noodles / fried noodle dishes (GI range 47–58, mean ~53). Stir-frying with oil, egg, and seafood does not substantially alter the GI of the noodle base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 64.75 g) / 100 ≈ 34.3, rounded to 34.2. Available carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g/100 g × 350 g = 64.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100 g; ~1820 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Maggi Goreng Seafood due to its high sodium content (~1820 mg per serving, representing approximately 79% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg), primarily from instant noodle seasoning, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; requesting reduced-sauce preparation or sharing a portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~53) and high glycemic load per serving (~34)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Maggi Goreng Seafood in moderation; although the GI is in the low-to-medium range (~53), the large carbohydrate load per standard serving (~65 g available carbohydrate) produces a high glycemic load (~34), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion reduction or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from frying oil and seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Maggi Goreng Seafood poses a moderate risk due to its high sodium content (~1820 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat (~4.2 g per serving); the seafood component provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but the overall sodium and fat profile warrants portion control and infrequent consumption as part of a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maggi Goreng Seafood is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean hawker dish consisting of Maggi brand instant noodles stir-fried in a hot wok with mixed seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish cake — along with egg, bean sprouts, spring onion, and chilli. The dish is seasoned with a combination of soy sauce, oyster sauce, and chilli sauce, giving it a savoury, slightly spicy, and umami-rich flavour profile. The noodles are characteristically springy and slightly charred from high-heat wok cooking, while the seafood adds a briny sweetness and textural contrast. Maggi Goreng Seafood is a variant of the broader Mee Goreng family and is closely related to Indian-Muslim Mee Goreng, which uses a tomato-based sauce; the seafood version is more common in Chinese-operated hawker stalls and casual Malaysian-Singaporean restaurants. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g plate provides approximately 518 kcal, 24.5 g protein, 19.3 g fat, and 64.8 g carbohydrate, with a high sodium content (~1820 mg) due to the seasoning sauces and instant noodle flavour packet. The dish has a moderate glycemic index (~53) but a high glycemic load per serving (~34), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. It is not suitable for those with shellfish, mollusc, gluten, soy, or egg allergies. The dish is generally considered halal when prepared with halal-certified seafood and sauces.

What seafood is in Maggi Goreng Seafood?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Maggi Goreng Seafood exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Mee Goreng (fried instant noodles) entry with cross-reference to IMR Malaysia MyFCD Mee Goreng, with minor adjustments for the seafood protein component. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using cooked instant noodles as the analogous food. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo 'Mee Goreng' and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD 'Mee Goreng'; no exact Maggi Goreng Seafood entry was found in any approved primary source. 2. Sodium is high (~520 mg/100 g) due to instant noodle seasoning, soy sauce, and oyster sauce; actual values vary by restaurant and preparation. 3. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 5. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or IMR MyFCD source entries for this dish category. 9. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (cooked instant noodles / fried noodle dishes, GI range 47–58); no direct GI measurement for Maggi Goreng Seafood was found. 10. Halal status assumes use of halal-certified seafood and sauces, which is typical but not guaranteed at all outlets. 11. The dish is served at 2 field survey data restaurants (Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant - Circular Road; Baba Eating House - South Bridge Road) at SGD 8.00–9.20.
